active_paradigms.idea: Introduce a state-cooperativity exponent on the growth term to control sigmoid asymmetry and lag sharpness (Richards/generalized-logistic capacity), directly targeting the 25% shape loss instead of the near-saturated NMSE.
active_paradigms.description: Keep the source structure (seed + primary + elongation, inhibitor divisor) but replace the linear capacity (plateau - c) with a generalized Richards capacity that reshapes onset sharpness: expression = (seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elong) * (plateau - c) * (c + eps) / inhibitor_scale, i.e. multiply the rate by c itself so growth is autocatalytic-cooperative (dc/dt vanishes at c=0 unless seeded, producing a genuine lag then sharp rise). Concretely build growth = c[2]**2*monomer*concentration*(concentration + c[6]) to add a controllable quadratic self-acceleration, and keep capacity = plateau - concentration. The (concentration + small_seed) factor makes the derivative small at low c (lag phase) and peak near mid-c (sharp rise), matching amyloid asymmetry and the half-response timing the shape term measures. Use 7 constants: c0..c5 as now plus c6 (a small baseline seeding offset, squared to stay positive) so the M0=0 primary-nucleation channel still ignites. Feature scaling (1e6) unchanged. initial_values length must match constant count; set the c6 start near 0.05. This is fundamentally different from failed attempts because they modified the SOURCE/inhibitor terms while the shape mismatch lives in the STATE nonlinearity of the capacity/growth coupling.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss (slope profile + half-response/onset timing), which is 25% of scored_loss and where failed source-term edits gained nothing; secondarily heldout_curve_shape_loss.
active_paradigms.cautions: Do not add more than one new constant to avoid overfitting small datasets. Ensure (concentration + c6**2) stays strictly positive; c is normalized in [0,1] so no singularity. Keep the (plateau - c) capacity to preserve mass conservation and cd=0 limit. Only ONE evaluate_expression call, straight-line code, no Piecewise/Max. Verify initial_values has exactly 7 entries matching constant_symbols(7).

active_paradigms.idea: Replace the multiplicative inhibitor rate-divisor with an inhibitor-shifted effective time/threshold inside the growth trigger, so cd suppresses the ONSET timing (half-time) rather than uniformly scaling the whole rate — targeting the timing component of the shape loss.
active_paradigms.description: The shape loss explicitly compares half-response/onset timing; a pure rate divisor shifts the whole curve but cannot independently tune WHEN growth ignites vs how steep it is. Restructure so the inhibitor raises the autocatalytic ignition threshold: define effective_state = concentration / (1 + c[3]**2 * inhibitor) meaning inhibitor makes the aggregate 'feel' less advanced, delaying the point where autocatalysis takes off. Then growth = (c[0]**2*seed + c[1]**2*monomer + c[2]**2*monomer*effective_state) and expression = growth * (plateau - concentration). Here the capacity uses true concentration (mass conservation preserved, halts at plateau) but the autocatalytic drive uses the inhibitor-attenuated effective_state, so higher cd delays the sharp-rise phase and shifts t_half without flattening the final slope — decoupling timing from steepness. Use 5 constants (c0..c4 plateau), fewer than current, reducing overfit risk. inhibitor = x[3]*1e6, denominator 1+positive is singularity-free, cd=0 gives effective_state=concentration exactly (clean uninhibited limit). This differs from the failed 'time-delayed source coupling' because the delay is applied to the STATE feedback of autocatalysis, not as an additive onset shift to the source, giving a smooth self-consistent lag.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: half-response/onset timing portion of shape_loss and heldout extrapolation across cd values (heldout_curve_shape_loss), while keeping NMSE stable.
active_paradigms.cautions: Denominator (1 + c3**2*inhibitor) must stay >0 (guaranteed). Keep plateau as bounded capacity so trajectory cannot overshoot 1. Do not combine with the elongation divisor to avoid double-counting inhibition. Exactly one evaluate_expression call; initial_values length = 5. Watch that fewer constants still fit high-cd curves — verify no dataset produces non-finite integration.

active_paradigms.idea: Adopt a Gompertz-in-state formulation using log-capacity ln(plateau/(c+eps)) as the growth driver, giving intrinsic front-loaded asymmetry that matches amyloid lag-then-plateau shapes better than the symmetric logistic (plateau-c) capacity.
active_paradigms.description: Amyloid aggregation curves are strongly asymmetric: long flat lag, rapid rise, gradual approach to plateau. The logistic (plateau-c) is symmetric about its midpoint and mismatches this, which the shape loss (slope profile) penalizes. Use a Gompertz capacity that is analytic and smooth: capacity_g = sp.log((plateau + c[5]**2) / (concentration + c[5]**2)) where c[5]**2 is a tiny positive regularizer keeping the log argument strictly positive over c in [0,1] and plateau near 0.92. Then expression = (c[0]**2*seed + c[1]**2*monomer + c[2]**2*monomer*concentration) * capacity_g / inhibitor_scale with inhibitor_scale = 1 + c[3]**2*inhibitor (simple saturating linear divisor, since the Gompertz already handles asymmetry and we avoid the failed Hill complexity). The log-ratio drives fast growth when c is small relative to plateau and smoothly vanishes as c->plateau, producing the characteristic Gompertz asymmetry and later t_half. Use 6 constants (c0 seed, c1 primary, c2 elong, c3 inhibitor, c4 plateau, c5 log-regularizer). Note: unlike the FAILED 'Gompertz-type growth' attempt which replaced the symmetric logistic capacity wholesale and likely destabilized fitting, here the log-ratio is regularized with c5**2 for numerical safety AND retains the additive nucleation channels and inhibitor divisor structure that already work — a minimal, well-conditioned change rather than a full reformulation.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: slope-profile component of shape_loss via asymmetric front-loaded kinetics; keep NMSE near current level; improve robustness on high-cd held-out curves.
active_paradigms.cautions: sp.log argument must be strictly positive: since plateau (~0.92) and c<=~1, set c5 initial ~0.3 so c5**2~0.09 guarantees positivity even if c momentarily exceeds plateau during integration; monitor for non-finite values. This is the ONE risky operation — the c5**2 offset in both numerator and denominator of the ratio prevents log(0) and log(negative). Exactly one evaluate_expression call, straight-line, no conditionals. initial_values length = 6. Prefer this only if idea 1 and 2 stall, since log can complicate least-squares conditioning.

tried_paradigms.idea: Add surface-catalyzed secondary-nucleation term (m0*c^2) with inhibitor acting selectively on that autocatalytic channel
tried_paradigms.description: Replace the purely linear autocatalytic elongation with the Knowles two-step form: keep linear elongation k2^2*m0*c but ADD a secondary (surface) nucleation term k5^2*m0*concentration**2, which is the physically dominant sigmoid-sharpening mechanism in amyloid kinetics. Make the inhibitor suppress ONLY the autocatalytic channels (elongation + secondary nucleation) via a shared 1/(1+k3^2*cd) factor, while leaving the seed/primary nucleation source uninhibited. Physically: many Abeta inhibitors block fibril-surface catalysis rather than de-novo nucleation, so the inhibitor changes the sigmoid steepness/half-time (shape), not the initiation floor. Final RHS: expression = (plateau - c)*( seed_nuc + primary_nuc + (k2^2*m0*c + k5^2*m0*c^2)/(1 + k3^2*cd) ). Use 6 constants (k0..k5) plus plateau => 7 total, well under 13. All rates squared for sign-definiteness; c is bounded in [0,~1] so c^2 <= c, no overflow. Denominator >=1 so no singularities. This targets the shape_loss (25% weight) by producing sharper, better-timed onset while preserving cd=0 reduction.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: shape_loss (slope profile + half-response timing) and heldout_curve_shape_loss, while keeping NMSE stable
tried_paradigms.cautions: Keep the secondary term multiplied by m0 (available monomer) so it saturates as monomer depletes implicitly via plateau factor; do not let both linear and quadratic autocatalytic terms become collinear — initialize k5 small (e.g. 0.3) and k2 moderate. Verify cd=0 curves still initiate via uninhibited primary_nucleation. initial_values same as current [0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92].

tried_paradigms.idea: Inhibitor delays onset via a saturating (Hill-like) suppression 1/(1+ (k3*cd)^2) plus cd-dependent plateau reduction
tried_paradigms.description: Two structural changes targeting inhibitor physics. First, use a sharper cooperative suppression denominator: inhibitor_scale = 1 + k3^2*cd + k5^2*cd**2, a quadratic-in-cd term that captures stoichiometric/cooperative binding where doubling inhibitor more than doubles delay (common for Abeta binders with multiple sites). This bends the half-time-vs-cd curve to match the measured monotone rise from ~0.2 to ~0.7 more accurately than the linear-only denominator. Second, let the plateau depend weakly on cd: plateau = c4 / (1 + k6^2*cd), allowing inhibitors that reduce final aggregate mass while still reducing cleanly to c4 at cd=0. Keep the reduced master-equation core: (plateau - c)*(seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elongation). This is a global-rate/plateau modification rather than a new kinetic channel. Total 7 constants. Denominators are strictly >=1 (squared coefficients, cd>=0) so no singularities and no non-real values.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: half-response timing shape term for high-cd curves and any datasets where inhibitor lowers plateau, improving both NMSE and shape on inhibited curves
tried_paradigms.cautions: The quadratic-in-cd term can dominate at large cd; since cd is scaled by 1e6 it is order-one, so keep k5 initialized small (0.2). Ensure plateau stays >0.5 typically by keeping k6 small init (0.1); a plateau collapsing below the observed data would hurt NMSE. Watch collinearity between k3 and k5 in the denominator — they are separable because one is linear one quadratic.

tried_paradigms.idea: Reformulate as a Gompertz-type growth (asymmetric sigmoid) driven by an inhibitor-delayed effective rate, replacing the symmetric logistic capacity
tried_paradigms.description: The logistic capacity (plateau - c) is symmetric about its inflection, but amyloid aggregation curves are typically ASYMMETRIC with a long lag and rapid rise — better captured by Gompertz kinetics. Use a capacity factor of c*(sp.log(plateau) - sp.log(c + eps)) style is unstable; instead use the smooth Gompertz-equivalent rate driven directly: expression = (plateau - c) * ( source + growth ) where growth = k2^2 * m0 * c * (plateau - c)  gives a steeper, later-peaking slope (a cubic-in-state shape that mimics Gompertz asymmetry) while remaining polynomial and singularity-free. Combined with seed/primary source terms k0^2*M0 + k1^2*m0, and multiplicative inhibitor 1/(1+k3^2*cd). This keeps everything polynomial/rational (no logs, no roots, no overflow), c bounded so (plateau-c)^2 is bounded. The extra (plateau-c) factor on the autocatalytic term shifts the inflection point later, directly reducing onset-timing shape_loss for lag-dominated curves. 5 constants total, matching current parsimony.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: onset/lag timing and slope-profile shape_loss via asymmetric (later-inflection) sigmoid, without adding constants
tried_paradigms.cautions: Do NOT use sp.log or sqrt (instability, non-real). The (plateau-c)^2-like factor makes growth vanish faster near plateau — verify curves still reach plateau by ensuring the constant source (seed_nuc+primary_nuc)*(plateau-c) drives the final approach. Initialize plateau=0.92; if the asymmetry over-delays onset, the multi-start on initial_values should recover it. Keep initial_values=[0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92].

tried_paradigms.idea: Add secondary-nucleation c^2 autocatalytic term with inhibitor targeting only the autocatalytic channels (Knowles/Cohen master equation reduced form)
tried_paradigms.description: Replace the single linear elongation with two growth channels: elongation ~ c2^2*monomer*c (linear addition) and secondary_nucleation ~ c5^2*monomer*c**2 (autocatalytic, the sharp-sigmoid driver). Keep seed_nucleation ~ c0^2*seed and primary_nucleation ~ c1^2*monomer as c-independent sources for the lag/onset. Crucially, apply inhibitor suppression ONLY to the autocatalytic (secondary + elongation) channels, since experimental amyloid inhibitors (e.g. Brichos) selectively block secondary nucleation while leaving primary nucleation nearly untouched: expression = capacity * ( (seed_nuc + primary_nuc) + (elongation + secondary_nuc)/(1 + c3^2*inhibitor + c6^2*inhibitor**2) ). capacity = plateau - c with plateau=c4. Use 7 constants (c0..c6). The c**2 term gives a steeper onset that better matches half-response/onset shape loss, and channel-specific inhibition lets cd delay timing without artificially suppressing the seed-free initiation. cd=0 recovers full uninhibited kinetics exactly. All rates squared so >=0, denominator >=1 (no singularity). initial_values=[0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92,0.3,0.5].
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss (half-response/onset timing) via sharper c^2 sigmoid, while preserving NMSE; channel-specific inhibition improves high-cd curve timing
tried_paradigms.cautions: c**2 with c in [0,1] stays bounded; keep plateau slightly above 1 so capacity stays positive during integration. 7 constants still well under 13. Ensure secondary term does not dominate cd=0 lag (primary_nuc kept outside inhibition). Watch that the extra constant does not overfit small datasets — squared coeffs keep it monotone.

tried_paradigms.idea: Hill-type saturable inhibitor binding replacing the polynomial denominator, capturing cooperative saturation of inhibitor delay
tried_paradigms.description: Keep the current three source channels (seed, primary, elongation) but replace the polynomial inhibitor_scale = 1 + k*cd + k2*cd^2 with a saturable Hill-like slowing factor that models finite inhibitor binding sites: inhibitor_scale = 1 + c3^2 * inhibitor**2 / (c5^2 + inhibitor). This is a Michaelis/Hill-2 form: at small cd it rises quadratically (matching the observed super-linear early delay 0.077->0.10->0.14), but at large cd it transitions toward linear/saturating growth rather than unbounded quadratic, preventing over-delay extrapolation on the highest-cd held-out curves. expression = capacity*(seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elongation)/inhibitor_scale, plateau=c4, capacity=plateau-c. The denominator c5^2 + inhibitor is strictly positive (c5^2>0, inhibitor>=0), so no singularity; the whole scale is >=1. This targets robustness/extrapolation on held-out high-cd curves where a pure quadratic over-predicts delay. Uses 6 constants (same count as current). initial_values=[0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92,0.3].
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: heldout_curve_shape_loss and heldout_curve_nmse on high-cd extrapolation; prevents quadratic over-delay while keeping small-cd super-linear behavior
tried_paradigms.cautions: Ensure c5^2 fitted away from zero via reasonable init (0.3) so denominator well-conditioned; the Hill form is smooth everywhere for inhibitor>=0. Same constant count avoids added overfitting risk. Verify cd=0 gives scale=1 exactly.

tried_paradigms.idea: Explicit lag-phase via time-delayed source coupling: inhibitor shifts onset time additively rather than only scaling rate
tried_paradigms.description: Introduce a lag/onset mechanism where the effective driving source is gated by an accumulating self-limiting factor of the current state, and let cd raise the activation threshold. Structure: growth = capacity * (seed_nuc + primary_nuc + elongation) where elongation ~ c2^2*monomer*c, and multiply the autocatalytic buildup by a smooth self-accelerating factor (c + c6^2) representing minimal nucleated mass, divided by inhibitor threshold shift: expression = capacity*( c0^2*seed + c1^2*monomer + c2^2*monomer*c*(c + c6^2) ) / (1 + c3^2*inhibitor). The (c + c6^2) factor makes elongation start slow (small c) and accelerate — a physically grounded latency that sharpens the sigmoid onset without needing c**2 explicitly, and the small offset c6^2 keeps a nonzero seed of growth. This directly attacks the shape/onset timing term by producing a genuine lag. plateau=c4, capacity=plateau-c. 7 constants, all squared/positive; denominator>=1. cd=0 recovers uninhibited kinetics. initial_values=[0.5,0.05,0.5,1.0,0.92,0.3,0.1].
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss onset/half-response timing via genuine lag phase from state-dependent autocatalytic activation
tried_paradigms.cautions: Keep c6^2 init small (0.1) so the lag is expressed but growth still initiates from seed/primary terms; verify c stays in [0,1] so (c+c6^2) bounded. Ensure the product term does not create numerical stiffness at large capacity — plateau near 0.92-1.0 keeps it moderate. 7 constants under limit; watch for redundancy between primary_nuc and the offset c6^2.

tried_paradigms.idea: Multiplicative cooperative capacity: replace linear (P-c) with (P-c)*(a+c) autocatalytic saturating factor to produce a true Cohen-Knowles sigmoidal lag-burst-plateau, directly targeting shape_loss
tried_paradigms.description: The current linear capacity (P-c) yields near-symmetric logistic kinetics with a weak lag phase, which the shape_loss (slope-profile + onset timing) penalizes. From the amyloid master equation, aggregate mass grows as m(t)~cosh-type with an explicit exponential lag governed by autocatalytic feedback proportional to existing aggregate. Encode this by making growth self-reinforcing near c=0 yet capped near c=P: set expression = (plateau - concentration) * (c_small + concentration) * (k0^2*seed + k1^2*monomer) / inhibitor_scale, where c_small = c[6]^2 is a small positive nucleation floor keeping dc/dt>0 at c=0 (avoids a stuck fixed point) and (a+c) supplies quadratic autocatalysis that sharpens the burst and lengthens the lag. Keep the existing saturable inhibitor_scale = 1 + c[3]^2*inhibitor^2/(c[5]^2+inhibitor) and squared constants for sign-definiteness. This uses one extra constant (7 total) but converts symmetric growth into asymmetric sigmoid without a fragile Gompertz log. Keep initial_values with a small floor like 0.02 for c[6]. The product (P-c)*(a+c) is a smooth quadratic, globally bounded, no singularities.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: dataset_shape_loss (slope profile and half-response/onset timing) while preserving nmse; improves heldout_curve_shape_loss which is the primary generalization diagnostic
tried_paradigms.cautions: Do not let a+c degenerate: keep c_small>0 via squared constant so dc/dt is nonzero at c=0. Ensure plateau initial value ~0.92 and c_small init small (~0.03). Watch that the quadratic in c does not overshoot plateau during odeint (it cannot since (P-c) forces the derivative to 0 at c=P). Keep total constants under 13 (7 used).

tried_paradigms.idea: Time-gated effective rate: multiply the growth rate by a smooth saturating time ramp x0/(tau+x0) so the model represents an emergent nucleation lag whose onset shifts with inhibitor, directly matching half-time timing in shape_loss
tried_paradigms.description: Rather than reshaping capacity, make the effective aggregation rate ramp in over normalized time so the flat lag phase is explicit. amyloid lag time scales with nucleation barriers and is pushed later by inhibitors. Define time_gate = x0^2 / (tau^2 + x0^2) with tau = c[6]^2 (a smooth Hill-in-time, 0 at t=0 rising to 1), so early derivative is suppressed producing a genuine lag, then growth engages. Fold the inhibitor into the lag onset by setting tau_eff shifted by inhibitor: tau2 = c[5]^2 + c[6]^2*inhibitor, then time_gate = x0^2/(tau2 + x0^2). Full RHS = (plateau - concentration) * (k0^2*seed + k1^2*monomer + k2^2*monomer*concentration) * time_gate. Because inhibitor now delays the gate onset (later half-time) rather than merely scaling amplitude, it captures the observed t_half shift (0.077->0.14) as a pure timing effect, which is exactly what shape_loss measures. All denominators strictly positive (squared constants + nonneg inhibitor). x0 is min-max normalized to [0,1] so x0^2 is well conditioned. Uses 7 constants.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: half-response/onset timing component of shape_loss and heldout extrapolation across inhibitor doses; keeps nmse via retained elongation term
tried_paradigms.cautions: x0 is normalized per-curve so t=0 truly gives gate=0; ensure initial_values give tau^2 around 0.01-0.05 so the lag is short enough not to kill fast curves. Avoid making the gate too steep (large tau) which flattens everything. Since dc/dt->0 as x0->0, the seed/primary nucleation still needs enough weight for seeded curves that start rising immediately; keep c2 elongation to preserve autocatalysis.

tried_paradigms.idea: Fractional-order autocatalytic capacity using a stable power sqrt/exponent-free surrogate: capacity (P-c) raised to a fitted-but-bounded cooperativity via (P-c)*(P-c+eps) and monomer-consumption coupling to enforce mass conservation between free monomer and aggregate
tried_paradigms.description: Impose true mass conservation: free monomer depletes as aggregate grows, so the elongation driving force should be (m0-based available monomer) not fixed m0. Model available monomer as (monomer_scaled * (plateau - concentration)) i.e. monomer is consumed proportionally to converted mass, coupling growth to a shrinking substrate pool and naturally producing the burst-then-decelerate asymmetry. RHS = (plateau - concentration) * (k0^2*seed + k1^2*monomer*(plateau-concentration) + k2^2*monomer*concentration*(plateau-concentration)) / inhibitor_scale. This makes the primary-nucleation and elongation channels doubly capacity-gated (mass-conserving substrate depletion), giving a sharper sigmoid with a longer lag and faster late saturation than a single (P-c) factor, without any log/power/exp that risks non-real values. Keep the saturable inhibitor_scale unchanged and squared constants. This is a first-principles conservation change (substrate + product = const) rather than a phenomenological reshape. Reuse the 6-constant budget, no new constants, reducing overfitting risk. Test whether the double-gating on elongation over-slows late growth; if so keep only primary nucleation double-gated.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: shape_loss asymmetry and nmse simultaneously via physically grounded substrate depletion; strong robustness on small datasets due to unchanged constant count
tried_paradigms.cautions: The double (P-c) factor squares the deceleration near plateau which can make late-time fit too flat; verify plateau init ~0.95 so the curve still reaches the data max. Ensure derivative stays positive: all factors (P-c)>0 within [0,P] and squared constants guarantee sign. No singularities since no division by variable-dependent zeros. Keep monomer scaling *1e6 for conditioning.
